text: The surgeon begins dissection near the sigmoid colon, which appears to be the most normal area, moving downward to address extensive adhesions that obscure the uterus.
The surgeon palpates the uterus through dense adhesions, while the bladder is visibly adherent to the bowel in the center of the surgical field.
The surgeon makes bold incisions on the bladder, which is visible and slightly easier to repair compared to the bowel.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and gently lateralizes it. The infundibulopelvic ligament is then located, with the infundibulopelvic vein or gonadal vein visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic shearer and a suction cannula to perform dissection. The suction cannula acts as a blunt dissector, effectively separating structures without causing trauma.
The surgeon gently separates the identified infundibulopelvic vein from the pararectal space, creating additional space to access the deeper pararectal areas.

The surgeon dissects para sigmoidal fat away from the ovarian adhesion, revealing the left side ovarian tissue with visible endometriotic changes. The sigmoid colon is observed plastered to the bladder.
The surgeon cuts into the parasigmoid fat plane, which is the initial site of adhesions. As the dissection progresses, the serosal aspect becomes visible and adherent, allowing for easier separation.

The appendix is visibly adherent to the ovary and sigmoid colon. The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate the appendix from these structures, highlighting the severe engagement of the appendix within the pelvic region.
The surgeon decompresses a chocolate cyst, releasing its contents and creating additional space within the pelvic cavity.

The surgeon identifies dense adhesions plastered between anatomical structures. Suture material, likely proline, is visible over the ovarian tissue, suggesting it was used to repair a sigmoid rent during a previous surgery.

The surgeon continues dissection without a clear anatomical plane, using the appendix as a reference point to navigate towards the ovary, and subsequently using the ovary to safely approach the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon identifies a part of the bladder and begins dissection by making a bold incision over it. The round ligament is visible and is used as a guide for performing the hysterectomy, with the presumed uterus located beneath the bladder.

The surgeon identifies the round ligament and opens the spaces below it, revealing the paravesical space for increased mobilization. The uterine origin and ureter are visible despite the presence of dense adhesions.

The surgeon performs further dissection to separate the bladder from the uterus. The fundus of the uterus is visible, showing destruction due to endometriosis. The bladder is identified, and bold cuts are made over its dome to facilitate separation.
The surgeon identifies endometriotic tissue, releasing chocolate-colored fluid from the affected area during dissection.

The surgeon has successfully separated the bladder from the uterus, revealing the cervix and the vagina below. The round ligament serves as a guide for further dissection in the anterior plane to achieve separation of these structures.

The surgeon uses laparoscopic instruments to compress tissue, distinguishing between the uterus, bladder, and bowel. The ureter is carefully lateralized away from the fibrosed pararectal plane, revealing the ovarian remnant.

The surgeon identifies peritoneal tissue near the ovary as a landmark to begin dissection into the pararectal space. The dissection aims to mobilize the rectum and sigmoid colon from both lateral sides, a crucial step in managing endometriosis. The appendix is visible leading into the pelvis.

The surgeon lifts the appendix to reveal the underlying space, aiming to identify the ureter. This maneuver is expected to open a new dissection plane on the lateral aspect, similar to the opposite side.

The surgeon uses a shearer to dissect dense fibrotic tissue around the sigmoid colon, revealing suture material from a previous suspected injury. The appendix is lifted, allowing identification of the ureter, which facilitates lateral dissection. The remaining attachment is along the central aspect of the bowel.

The surgeon carefully separates the deep pararectal space while keeping the ureter in mind, aiming to detach the bowel from this plane. The dissection progresses centrally, completing the bowel separation on both sides. The use of 3D laparoscopy enhances depth perception during this intricate procedure.

The surgeon addresses the central aspect where the bowel is adherent, using either a cold knife or laparoscopic shearer to make bold cuts. These cuts rapidly mobilize the upper part of the rectum, which is adherent to the posterior aspect of the uterus. The surgeon may shave off a small part of the uterus on the anterior aspect of the rectum to prevent injury.
The surgeon completes significant separation of adhesions, reaching the level of the utero-sacral ligaments. Despite the distortion of the uterus, the hysterectomy proceeds with standard steps.
A nodule is visible at the posterior cervix, which is being dissected as part of the surgery. The hysterectomy, including salpingo-oophorectomy, is completed off-screen.

The surgeon enters the Denonvilliers fascia and identifies the plane, applying traction to the anterior aspect of the rectum. A nodularity is revealed and separated using traction, counter traction, and electrocoagulation with a laparoscopic shearer. This process frees the bowel from the pelvis and opens the pouch of Douglas.

The surgeon uses bold cuts to separate fibrotic adhesions between the pararectal area and the posterior aspect of the cervix. Traction and counter traction are applied using a suction cannula to facilitate dissection. A point adhesion is liberated, allowing the bowel to be lifted out of the pelvis, freeing the pouch of Douglas and enabling colpotomy for hysterectomy completion.

The surgeon completes the central dissection and proceeds with the colpotomy using a laparoscopic shearer. The distorted uterus is removed vaginally from the peritoneal cavity. Vault closure is performed, and an appendicectomy is conducted due to the appendix being plastered inside the pelvis. A small vaginal nodule is identified and liberated during the colpotomy. A drain is placed in the abdomen due to intensive dissection at the small and large bowel levels.
